title: CDMGRestrictions
tex_block_html: With the notations not-cdmg the restrictions in definition def-cdmg mean that the nodes $j \in J$ will not have any arrowheads pointing towards them: $ j \hus v \notin G$. Nodes $j \in J$ can only point towards nodes $v \in V$: edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed. Furthermore, no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent.

tex_statement.raw:
% Claim statement file for row claim_3_1 -- "CDMGRestrictions".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker it dispatches) fills the body below with the
% claim's statement(s) from the lecture notes -- statement only, no proof
% (proofs live in the sibling `claim_3_1_proof_*.tex` file).
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it render both standalone and as part of
% `main.tex`. Note: `main.tex` does NOT \subfile this statement file -- the
% sibling `_proof_` file restates the statement above the proof, so
% including both in `main.tex` would be redundant. This file exists for
% standalone reading / grep convenience.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_1 (statement)
% title: CDMGRestrictions
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_1}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_1}

\begin{Rem}[CDMGRestrictions]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); we use the notation of \ref{not-cdmg} throughout. Recall, per def \ref{def-cdmg}, that $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, that $L \subseteq V \times V$ with $L$ symmetric and irreflexive, and that $J \cap V = \emptyset$.

    The restrictions encoded in def \ref{def-cdmg} have the following three consequences. In the LN wording, the clauses ``$j \hus v \notin G$'' and ``edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed'' carry an implicit universal quantification over the second-position variable $v$; we surface these quantifiers explicitly below, with $v$ ranging over $J \cup V$ in the first clause and over $V$ only in the second (see also the addition spelled out under sub-claim ii.).

    \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]
        \item \emph{No edge with an arrowhead at a $J$-node.}
              For every $j \in J$ and every $v \in J \cup V$,
              \[ j \hus v \notin G. \]
              Unfolding the shorthand $\hus$ via def \ref{not-cdmg} item~6 (so $j \hus v \in G$ abbreviates $(v, j) \in E \lor (j, v) \in L$), this is equivalent to the set-theoretic conjunction
              \[ (v, j) \notin E \;\land\; (j, v) \notin L. \]

        \item \emph{The type of $G$ does not forbid a directed edge from a $J$-node into a $V$-node.}
              For every $j \in J$ and every $v \in V$, the ordered pair $(j, v)$ satisfies $j \in J \cup V$ and $v \in V$, so the typing constraint $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ of def \ref{def-cdmg} is compatible with $(j, v) \in E$; equivalently, no restriction in def \ref{def-cdmg} forbids the inclusion of $(j, v)$ in $E$ (in the notation of \ref{not-cdmg}: no restriction forbids $j \tuh v \in G$). This is a \emph{permission} statement, not an existence statement: $(j, v) \in E$ is admissible by the definition for every $(j, v) \in J \times V$, but is not asserted to hold.

        \item \emph{No two $J$-nodes are adjacent.}
              For every $j_1 \in J$ and every $j_2 \in J$, the nodes $j_1$ and $j_2$ are \emph{not} adjacent in $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def-edge-relations} item~i.; equivalently,
              \[ (j_1, j_2) \notin E \;\land\; (j_2, j_1) \notin E \;\land\; (j_1, j_2) \notin L. \]
    \end{enumerate}
\end{Rem}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); we use the notation of not-cdmg throughout. Recall, per def def-cdmg, that $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, that $L \subseteq V \times V$ with $L$ symmetric and irreflexive, and that $J \cap V = \emptyset$.

The restrictions encoded in def def-cdmg have the following three consequences. In the LN wording, the clauses “$j \hus v \notin G$” and “edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed” carry an implicit universal quantification over the second-position variable $v$; we surface these quantifiers explicitly below, with $v$ ranging over $J \cup V$ in the first clause and over $V$ only in the second (see also the addition spelled out under sub-claim ii.).

  1. No edge with an arrowhead at a $J$-node. For every $j \in J$ and every $v \in J \cup V$, \[ j \hus v \notin G. \] Unfolding the shorthand $\hus$ via def not-cdmg item 6 (so $j \hus v \in G$ abbreviates $(v, j) \in E \lor (j, v) \in L$), this is equivalent to the set-theoretic conjunction \[ (v, j) \notin E \;\land\; (j, v) \notin L. \]
  2. The type of $G$ does not forbid a directed edge from a $J$-node into a $V$-node. For every $j \in J$ and every $v \in V$, the ordered pair $(j, v)$ satisfies $j \in J \cup V$ and $v \in V$, so the typing constraint $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ of def def-cdmg is compatible with $(j, v) \in E$; equivalently, no restriction in def def-cdmg forbids the inclusion of $(j, v)$ in $E$ (in the notation of not-cdmg: no restriction forbids $j \tuh v \in G$). This is a permission statement, not an existence statement: $(j, v) \in E$ is admissible by the definition for every $(j, v) \in J \times V$, but is not asserted to hold.
  3. No two $J$-nodes are adjacent. For every $j_1 \in J$ and every $j_2 \in J$, the nodes $j_1$ and $j_2$ are not adjacent in $G$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations item i.; equivalently, \[ (j_1, j_2) \notin E \;\land\; (j_2, j_1) \notin E \;\land\; (j_1, j_2) \notin L. \]

% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. A manager
% agent dedicated to writing the TeX proof fills in the proof body below.
%

%
% The statement is restated above the proof so the file is self-contained
% when rendered on its own. The proof must:

%     the LN first for a `\begin{proof}` block immediately following the
%     claim; copy / lightly edit when present).
%   - Otherwise use the LN paradigm and cite prior claims by their `ref`.
%   - Be self-contained enough that a mathematician can follow it without
%     re-reading the LN.
%   - Have no `sorry`, `omitted`, or "obvious" shortcuts.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_1 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: CDMGRestrictions
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_1}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_1}

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Rem}[CDMGRestrictions]
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); we use the notation of \ref{not-cdmg} throughout. Recall, per def \ref{def-cdmg}, that $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, that $L \subseteq V \times V$ with $L$ symmetric and irreflexive, and that $J \cap V = \emptyset$.

    The restrictions encoded in def \ref{def-cdmg} have the following three consequences. In the LN wording, the clauses ``$j \hus v \notin G$'' and ``edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed'' carry an implicit universal quantification over the second-position variable $v$; we surface these quantifiers explicitly below, with $v$ ranging over $J \cup V$ in the first clause and over $V$ only in the second (see also the addition spelled out under sub-claim ii.).

    \begin{enumerate}[label=\roman*.)]
        \item \emph{No edge with an arrowhead at a $J$-node.}
              For every $j \in J$ and every $v \in J \cup V$,
              \[ j \hus v \notin G. \]
              Unfolding the shorthand $\hus$ via def \ref{not-cdmg} item~6 (so $j \hus v \in G$ abbreviates $(v, j) \in E \lor (j, v) \in L$), this is equivalent to the set-theoretic conjunction
              \[ (v, j) \notin E \;\land\; (j, v) \notin L. \]

        \item \emph{The type of $G$ does not forbid a directed edge from a $J$-node into a $V$-node.}
              For every $j \in J$ and every $v \in V$, the ordered pair $(j, v)$ satisfies $j \in J \cup V$ and $v \in V$, so the typing constraint $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ of def \ref{def-cdmg} is compatible with $(j, v) \in E$; equivalently, no restriction in def \ref{def-cdmg} forbids the inclusion of $(j, v)$ in $E$ (in the notation of \ref{not-cdmg}: no restriction forbids $j \tuh v \in G$). This is a \emph{permission} statement, not an existence statement: $(j, v) \in E$ is admissible by the definition for every $(j, v) \in J \times V$, but is not asserted to hold.

        \item \emph{No two $J$-nodes are adjacent.}
              For every $j_1 \in J$ and every $j_2 \in J$, the nodes $j_1$ and $j_2$ are \emph{not} adjacent in $G$ in the sense of def \ref{def-edge-relations} item~i.; equivalently,
              \[ (j_1, j_2) \notin E \;\land\; (j_2, j_1) \notin E \;\land\; (j_1, j_2) \notin L. \]
    \end{enumerate}
\end{Rem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
\begin{proof}
All three sub-claims rest on the same underlying tool: the typing constraints carried by def \ref{def-cdmg}, namely $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, $L \subseteq V \times V$, and $J \cap V = \emptyset$. We prove each in turn.

\medskip
\emph{Proof of (i).}
Fix $j \in J$ and $v \in J \cup V$, and suppose, for contradiction, that $j \hus v \in G$. By def \ref{not-cdmg} item~6, the shorthand $j \hus v \in G$ abbreviates the disjunction
\[ (v, j) \in E \;\lor\; (j, v) \in L. \]
We rule out both disjuncts.
\begin{itemize}
    \item If $(v, j) \in E$, then by the typing constraint $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ of def \ref{def-cdmg}, the second component of any element of $E$ lies in $V$; hence $j \in V$. But $j \in J$ by assumption and $J \cap V = \emptyset$ (def \ref{def-cdmg}), so $j \in J \cap V = \emptyset$, a contradiction.
    \item If $(j, v) \in L$, then by the typing constraint $L \subseteq V \times V$ of def \ref{def-cdmg}, the first component of any element of $L$ lies in $V$; hence $j \in V$. The same contradiction $j \in J \cap V = \emptyset$ follows.
\end{itemize}
Both disjuncts therefore fail, so $j \hus v \notin G$. Unfolding the shorthand once more, this is the conjunction $(v, j) \notin E \land (j, v) \notin L$, as stated.

\medskip
\emph{Proof of (ii).}
Fix $j \in J$ and $v \in V$. We must show that the typing constraint $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ of def \ref{def-cdmg} is compatible with the ordered pair $(j, v)$ -- i.e.\ that $(j, v) \in (J \cup V) \times V$, so that nothing in def \ref{def-cdmg} forbids the membership $(j, v) \in E$. The membership $(j, v) \in (J \cup V) \times V$ is, by the definition of a Cartesian product, the conjunction
\[ j \in J \cup V \;\land\; v \in V. \]
The second conjunct is the standing hypothesis $v \in V$. For the first conjunct, $j \in J$ gives $j \in J \cup V$ by inclusion of the left summand into the union. Hence $(j, v) \in (J \cup V) \times V$, which is the typing precondition for an element of $E$. No further restriction in def \ref{def-cdmg} excludes the pair $(j, v)$ from $E$ (the only other constraints concern $L$, not $E$, and the disjointness $J \cap V = \emptyset$ is compatible with $(j, v)$ since $j \in J$ and $v \in V$ are unrelated by intersection). Thus the inclusion $(j, v) \in E$ is admissible by the definition, which is the permission statement claimed. (We emphasise that this argument does not assert that $(j, v) \in E$ holds for any particular CDMG: it asserts only that the definition of CDMG does not preclude it.)

\medskip
\emph{Proof of (iii).}
Fix $j_1 \in J$ and $j_2 \in J$, and suppose, for contradiction, that $j_1$ and $j_2$ are adjacent in $G$. By def \ref{def-edge-relations} item~i., adjacency unfolds to the disjunction
\[ (j_1, j_2) \in E \;\lor\; (j_2, j_1) \in E \;\lor\; (j_1, j_2) \in L. \]
We rule out all three disjuncts.
\begin{itemize}
    \item If $(j_1, j_2) \in E$, then $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ (def \ref{def-cdmg}) forces the second component $j_2 \in V$. But $j_2 \in J$, so $j_2 \in J \cap V = \emptyset$ (def \ref{def-cdmg}), a contradiction.
    \item If $(j_2, j_1) \in E$, the same constraint applied to the second component yields $j_1 \in V$; combined with $j_1 \in J$ this gives $j_1 \in J \cap V = \emptyset$, a contradiction.
    \item If $(j_1, j_2) \in L$, then $L \subseteq V \times V$ (def \ref{def-cdmg}) forces both components into $V$; in particular $j_1 \in V$, hence $j_1 \in J \cap V = \emptyset$, a contradiction. (The same step also yields $j_2 \in V$; either component on its own suffices.)
\end{itemize}
All three disjuncts therefore fail, so $j_1$ and $j_2$ are not adjacent; equivalently the conjunction $(j_1, j_2) \notin E \land (j_2, j_1) \notin E \land (j_1, j_2) \notin L$ holds, as stated.

The argument above does not separate the cases $j_1 = j_2$ and $j_1 \neq j_2$: the typing-driven contradictions apply uniformly. We note in passing that the diagonal case $j_1 = j_2 =: j$ also follows: the $E$-branches give $j \in V$ as above, and the $L$-branch additionally contradicts the irreflexivity of $L$ from def \ref{def-cdmg} (which forbids $(j, j) \in L$) on top of forcing $j \in V$. No diagonal subtlety arises.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Remark — CDMGRestrictions

Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); we use the notation of not-cdmg throughout. Recall, per def def-cdmg, that $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, that $L \subseteq V \times V$ with $L$ symmetric and irreflexive, and that $J \cap V = \emptyset$.

The restrictions encoded in def def-cdmg have the following three consequences. In the LN wording, the clauses “$j \hus v \notin G$” and “edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed” carry an implicit universal quantification over the second-position variable $v$; we surface these quantifiers explicitly below, with $v$ ranging over $J \cup V$ in the first clause and over $V$ only in the second (see also the addition spelled out under sub-claim ii.).

  1. No edge with an arrowhead at a $J$-node. For every $j \in J$ and every $v \in J \cup V$, \[ j \hus v \notin G. \] Unfolding the shorthand $\hus$ via def not-cdmg item 6 (so $j \hus v \in G$ abbreviates $(v, j) \in E \lor (j, v) \in L$), this is equivalent to the set-theoretic conjunction \[ (v, j) \notin E \;\land\; (j, v) \notin L. \]
  2. The type of $G$ does not forbid a directed edge from a $J$-node into a $V$-node. For every $j \in J$ and every $v \in V$, the ordered pair $(j, v)$ satisfies $j \in J \cup V$ and $v \in V$, so the typing constraint $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ of def def-cdmg is compatible with $(j, v) \in E$; equivalently, no restriction in def def-cdmg forbids the inclusion of $(j, v)$ in $E$ (in the notation of not-cdmg: no restriction forbids $j \tuh v \in G$). This is a permission statement, not an existence statement: $(j, v) \in E$ is admissible by the definition for every $(j, v) \in J \times V$, but is not asserted to hold.
  3. No two $J$-nodes are adjacent. For every $j_1 \in J$ and every $j_2 \in J$, the nodes $j_1$ and $j_2$ are not adjacent in $G$ in the sense of def def-edge-relations item i.; equivalently, \[ (j_1, j_2) \notin E \;\land\; (j_2, j_1) \notin E \;\land\; (j_1, j_2) \notin L. \]
Proof.

All three sub-claims rest on the same underlying tool: the typing constraints carried by def def-cdmg, namely $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, $L \subseteq V \times V$, and $J \cap V = \emptyset$. We prove each in turn.

Proof of (i). Fix $j \in J$ and $v \in J \cup V$, and suppose, for contradiction, that $j \hus v \in G$. By def not-cdmg item 6, the shorthand $j \hus v \in G$ abbreviates the disjunction \[ (v, j) \in E \;\lor\; (j, v) \in L. \] We rule out both disjuncts.

  • If $(v, j) \in E$, then by the typing constraint $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ of def def-cdmg, the second component of any element of $E$ lies in $V$; hence $j \in V$. But $j \in J$ by assumption and $J \cap V = \emptyset$ (def def-cdmg), so $j \in J \cap V = \emptyset$, a contradiction.
  • If $(j, v) \in L$, then by the typing constraint $L \subseteq V \times V$ of def def-cdmg, the first component of any element of $L$ lies in $V$; hence $j \in V$. The same contradiction $j \in J \cap V = \emptyset$ follows.

Both disjuncts therefore fail, so $j \hus v \notin G$. Unfolding the shorthand once more, this is the conjunction $(v, j) \notin E \land (j, v) \notin L$, as stated.

Proof of (ii). Fix $j \in J$ and $v \in V$. We must show that the typing constraint $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ of def def-cdmg is compatible with the ordered pair $(j, v)$ – i.e.\ that $(j, v) \in (J \cup V) \times V$, so that nothing in def def-cdmg forbids the membership $(j, v) \in E$. The membership $(j, v) \in (J \cup V) \times V$ is, by the definition of a Cartesian product, the conjunction \[ j \in J \cup V \;\land\; v \in V. \] The second conjunct is the standing hypothesis $v \in V$. For the first conjunct, $j \in J$ gives $j \in J \cup V$ by inclusion of the left summand into the union. Hence $(j, v) \in (J \cup V) \times V$, which is the typing precondition for an element of $E$. No further restriction in def def-cdmg excludes the pair $(j, v)$ from $E$ (the only other constraints concern $L$, not $E$, and the disjointness $J \cap V = \emptyset$ is compatible with $(j, v)$ since $j \in J$ and $v \in V$ are unrelated by intersection). Thus the inclusion $(j, v) \in E$ is admissible by the definition, which is the permission statement claimed. (We emphasise that this argument does not assert that $(j, v) \in E$ holds for any particular CDMG: it asserts only that the definition of CDMG does not preclude it.)

Proof of (iii). Fix $j_1 \in J$ and $j_2 \in J$, and suppose, for contradiction, that $j_1$ and $j_2$ are adjacent in $G$. By def def-edge-relations item i., adjacency unfolds to the disjunction \[ (j_1, j_2) \in E \;\lor\; (j_2, j_1) \in E \;\lor\; (j_1, j_2) \in L. \] We rule out all three disjuncts.

  • If $(j_1, j_2) \in E$, then $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$ (def def-cdmg) forces the second component $j_2 \in V$. But $j_2 \in J$, so $j_2 \in J \cap V = \emptyset$ (def def-cdmg), a contradiction.
  • If $(j_2, j_1) \in E$, the same constraint applied to the second component yields $j_1 \in V$; combined with $j_1 \in J$ this gives $j_1 \in J \cap V = \emptyset$, a contradiction.
  • If $(j_1, j_2) \in L$, then $L \subseteq V \times V$ (def def-cdmg) forces both components into $V$; in particular $j_1 \in V$, hence $j_1 \in J \cap V = \emptyset$, a contradiction. (The same step also yields $j_2 \in V$; either component on its own suffices.)

All three disjuncts therefore fail, so $j_1$ and $j_2$ are not adjacent; equivalently the conjunction $(j_1, j_2) \notin E \land (j_2, j_1) \notin E \land (j_1, j_2) \notin L$ holds, as stated.

The argument above does not separate the cases $j_1 = j_2$ and $j_1 \neq j_2$: the typing-driven contradictions apply uniformly. We note in passing that the diagonal case $j_1 = j_2 =: j$ also follows: the $E$-branches give $j \in V$ as above, and the $L$-branch additionally contradicts the irreflexivity of $L$ from def def-cdmg (which forbids $(j, j) \in L$) on top of forcing $j \in V$. No diagonal subtlety arises.

lean_blocks.code:
theorem no_arrowhead_into_J (G : CDMG Node) {j : Node} (hj : j ∈ G.J)
    {v : Node} (hv : v ∈ G.J ∪ G.V) :
    ¬ G.hus j v

theorem J_to_V_edge_admissible (G : CDMG Node) {j : Node} (hj : j ∈ G.J)
    {v : Node} (hv : v ∈ G.V) :
    j ∈ G.J ∪ G.V ∧ v ∈ G.V

theorem J_nodes_not_adjacent (G : CDMG Node) {j₁ : Node} (hj₁ : j₁ ∈ G.J)
    {j₂ : Node} (hj₂ : j₂ ∈ G.J) :
    ¬ G.adjacent j₁ j₂

addition_to_the_LN: [implicit_universal_quantifier_in_hus_clause] The statements "$j \hus v \notin G$" and "edges $j \tuh v$ are allowed" carry implicit universal quantification over $v$. Precisely: for every $j \in J$ and every node $v$ in the graph, $j \hus v \notin G$; and for every $j \in J$ and every $v \in V$, an edge $j \tuh v$ is permitted in $G$ (its presence is not asserted, only that no restriction forbids it).
design_choices:
**Split the remark into three independent theorems rather than a single conjunction.** The lecture notes group three consequences of the CDMG typing constraints into one numbered remark, so a tempting rendering is one theorem of the form `∀ j v, P₁ ∧ P₂ ∧ P₃`. That packaging was rejected because the three clauses are conceptually disjoint and downstream consumers will want them piecewise: walks (`def_3_4`) need (i) to rule out walks *ending* at a `J`-node, family relationships (`def_3_5`) combine (i) and (iii) to force `Pa^G(j) = ∅`, and hard intervention (`def_3_10`) leverages (ii) when promoting `V`-nodes to `J`-nodes. A monolithic conjunction would also force every consumer to destructure, and the three clauses bind different quantifier ranges anyway — clause (i) ranges $v$ over $J \cup V$ while clause (ii) ranges $v$ over $V$ only. Three theorems also matches the upstream style (`CDMGNotation.lean` splits its seven LN items, `EdgeRelations.lean` splits its three).

**State each clause via its named upstream relation, not the unfolded set-theoretic conjunction.** Clause (i) is rendered as `¬ G.hus j v` rather than the expansion `(v, j) ∉ G.E ∧ (j, v) ∉ G.L`, and clause (iii) as `¬ G.adjacent j₁ j₂` rather than the three-way conjunction over `G.E` and `G.L`. The unfolded form is closer to the rewrite's expansion sentence, but `CDMGNotation` and `EdgeRelations` exist precisely so that LN macros have a 1:1 Lean form; any consumer that needs the unfolded shape gets it with a single `unfold` step. Faithfulness to the LN macros wins over inlining their definitions.

**Render clause (ii) as the typing-constraint precondition `j ∈ G.J ∪ G.V ∧ v ∈ G.V`, not an extensibility statement.** Clause (ii) is a *permission* claim, and a closer reading of "permitted" suggests an extensibility witness `∃ G', G'.E = G.E ∪ {(j, v)} ∧ <still a valid CDMG>`. That form was rejected as heavier (it requires constructing a witness and re-proving every typing field) and because the canonical tex picked the lighter precondition form — exactly the RHS of `hE_subset`'s implication, instantiated at `(j, v)`. Consumers needing the extensibility version can derive it. For the same reason, the conclusion is written with set-membership rather than the `\tuh` notation: encoding `\tuh` would force the extensibility reading.

**Keep `Node` polymorphic with only `[DecidableEq Node]`, and wrap the `variable` line as a load-bearing `--- helper` block.** Fixing `Node` to a concrete carrier here would force every downstream chapter (CBNs, do-calculus, iSCMs, discovery) to renumber, so the chapter-wide convention is preserved. `[DecidableEq Node]` is the minimal typeclass inherited from `def_3_1` — needed for `Disjoint J V`, membership on `G.J ∪ G.V`, and unfolding `G.hus`. The `variable` line uses three-dash `--- start helper` / `--- end helper` markers (not the two-dash `-- start statement` form) because Lean's auto-binding makes it part of every theorem's signature: it is statement context, not throwaway sugar, and the helper marker flags that to reconciliation tooling. The whole file sits inside `namespace CDMG` to enable dot notation (`G.hus`, `G.adjacent`) that mirrors the LN's left-to-right macro reading.

**Implicit node binders, explicit membership hypotheses, and `G` first.** Each theorem makes `j`, `v` (or `j₁`, `j₂`) implicit while keeping the membership proofs `hj`, `hv` explicit — standard mathlib style so callers supply only the hypotheses and let Lean infer the witnesses. `G : CDMG Node` is the first explicit argument so dot-notation `G.no_arrowhead_into_J hj hv` reads naturally and matches the existing `G.adjacent`, `G.hE_subset` idiom.

**Omit a `j₁ ≠ j₂` precondition in clause (iii).** The LN says "no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent" without restricting to distinct nodes, and the diagonal case $j_1 = j_2$ is in fact covered: the `huh` self-loop is killed by `hL_irrefl`, and a directed self-loop `(j, j) ∈ G.E` would force $j \in G.V$ via `hE_subset`, contradicting `Disjoint G.J G.V`. Adding a distinctness hypothesis would weaken the theorem and drift away from the LN. Symmetry of `adjacent` is likewise left unstated: it follows from `sus`'s `tuh ∨ hut` flip plus `hL_symm`, and a one-line corollary suffices.
